title: Vegetarian shepherd's pie
subtitle: Green lentil and mushroom filling under a golden mashed potato crust
excerpt: A substantial vegetarian version of shepherd's pie built on green lentils, chestnut mushrooms, and a deeply savoury tomato gravy, topped with mashed potato and baked until the top is golden and the filling bubbles at the edges.
sourceNotes: Vegetarian lentil and mushroom versions of shepherd's pie became established in British home cooking through the 1980s and 1990s as vegetarian eating grew more common. The lentil base follows the method of the classic: braised in a well-seasoned gravy and topped with mashed potato.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze before the final bake. Defrost overnight, add 15 minutes to the oven time.
recipe.batchNotes: Doubles well. The filling can be made a day ahead.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Assemble up to 24 hours ahead, cover, and refrigerate. Bake from cold, adding 10 minutes.

content.text: The lentil filling needs to cook long enough that the lentils absorb the stock and the tomato gravy thickens around them. If the filling is at all wet when it goes into the dish, it will bubble over in the oven and the potato topping will slide. Simmer until the filling holds its shape when you drag a spoon through it.

Green lentils hold their shape better than red lentils, which turn to purée. Chestnut mushrooms add a meaty depth. Worcestershire sauce is the usual trick; use soy sauce for a fully vegan version. The potato topping should be generously buttered and slightly stiffer than serving mash, so it stays on top during baking.

Heat the {{olive-oil}} of olive oil in a large pot over medium heat. Add the {{onion}} onions and {{carrot}} carrots and cook for 10 minutes until softened. Add the {{garlic}} of garlic and cook for 1 minute. Add the {{chestnut-mushrooms}} of mushrooms and cook for 5 minutes until they release their liquid and begin to brown. Add the {{tomato-puree}} of tomato purée and stir for 1 minute. Add the {{tinned-tomatoes}} of tinned tomatoes, {{stock-vegetable}} of vegetable stock, {{lentils-green}} of rinsed lentils, {{worcestershire-sauce}} of Worcestershire sauce, and {{thyme-dried}} of dried thyme. Season with salt and pepper. Bring to the boil, then reduce the heat and simmer for 30 to 35 minutes, stirring occasionally, until the lentils are tender and the filling is thick. Taste and adjust the seasoning.

Boil the {{potato}} potatoes in salted water until tender, about 20 minutes. Drain and return to the pan. Add the {{unsalted-butter}} of butter and {{whole-milk}} of milk and mash until smooth. Season with salt and pepper.

Heat the oven to 200°C (fan 180°C). Spoon the filling into a baking dish. Spread the mash evenly over the top, running a fork across the surface to create ridges that will catch and brown. Bake for 25 to 30 minutes until the top is golden and the filling bubbles at the edges. Rest for 5 minutes before serving.

items.symptom: Filling is too liquid and bubbles through the mash
items.cause: Filling not reduced enough before assembling
items.fix: Simmer the filling until it holds its shape when a spoon is drawn through it; the mash will sink if the filling is too wet

items.symptom: Lentils are still tough after 35 minutes
items.cause: Acid from the tomatoes can slow lentil cooking
items.fix: Reduce the tinned tomatoes and add them in the last 10 minutes only; cook the lentils in stock first until nearly tender, then add the tomatoes to finish

items.cause: Mash too wet, or not enough ridges on the surface
items.fix: Make the mash slightly stiffer than usual; use a fork to create deep ridges; switch to the grill for the final 3 minutes if needed

content.text: Shepherd's pie without lamb is, in the strictest sense, not shepherd's pie. The name refers to the shepherd's ingredient: lamb. The vegetarian version found its way onto the British table during the 1980s, riding the growth of vegetarian cooking in the UK, and has since become a weeknight staple in its own right. The lentil base does the same job as the lamb mince: it provides body, absorbs flavour, and holds together under the potato. Whether it deserves the name is a question that can be left to the traditionalists.
